examples.title: The Atom Model
examples.content: We model atoms like little solar systems—electrons orbiting a nucleus. Is that true? Not exactly! Electrons don't really 'orbit.' But the model helps you visualize atoms. It's simple, works for most situations, but breaks down at advanced levels.

examples.title: The Ball as a Point
examples.content: In physics problems, we often treat a ball as a single point in space, ignoring its size and shape. Why? Because the size usually doesn't matter for calculating where it lands. The model simplifies without losing accuracy.

examples.title: Light as Rays
examples.content: We model light as straight rays bouncing and bending. Is light actually rays? No—it's waves (and photons). But the ray model works great for mirrors and lenses. A different model (wave) works better for explaining colors.
